summary.ascii_graph:
*-----.   Research three unrelated niche topics, extract the requested facts from each Wikipedia page, and then write a synthesis.

1. Cordyceps fungi: visit https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Cordyceps — find (a) the common name for the behaviour Cordyceps induces in ants, (b) one commercial or medicinal use of Cordyceps.

2. Admiralty law: visit https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Admiralty_law — find (a) the historical origin or earliest legal code associated with admiralty law, (b) one modern international maritime convention or treaty.

3. Mathematics of paper folding: visit https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Mathematics_of_paper_folding — find (a) the name of one theorem or axiom related to origami mathematics, (b) one practical engineering application of origami folding.

Finally, write a 150+ word essay identifying a UNIFYING THEME that connects all three topics (e.g., hidden complexity, emergent structure, nature inspiring engineering). The essay must reference specific facts from each topic. Cite the Wikipedia URL for every fact.

admiralty law

law

private international law

up to the third order.

final_output.final_deliverable:
## 1) Cordyceps fungi (https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Cordyceps)

### (a) Common name for the behaviour Cordyceps induces in ants
On the *Cordyceps* page, the behaviour is described in “zombie” terms. The page quotes *Scientific American* describing Cordyceps-like fungi as making “real zombies”:
- Verbatim evidence: Scientific American notes that some species in the genus **“are indeed body snatchers–they have been making real zombies for millions of years”** (ants are explicitly mentioned as hosts). (https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Cordyceps)

**Extracted common name:** **“real zombies”** (i.e., “zombie” behaviour in ants). (https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Cordyceps)

### (b) One commercial or medicinal use of Cordyceps
The page states that cordyceps has long been used in traditional Chinese medicine:
- Verbatim evidence: **“Diverse variants of cordyceps have had more than 1,500 years of use in Chinese medicine.”** (https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Cordyceps)
- Verbatim evidence (Uses section): **“Cordyceps sensu lato … has long been used in traditional Chinese medicine in the belief it can be used to treat diseases.”** (https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Cordyceps)

**Extracted use:** **Used in traditional Chinese medicine (as a purported treatment for diseases).** (https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Cordyceps)


## 2) Admiralty law (https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Admiralty_law)

### (a) Historical origin or earliest legal code associated with admiralty law
The page points to an ancient example: Rhodian law (*Nomos Rhodion Nautikos*):
- Verbatim evidence (History section): **“An ancient example was the Rhodian law (Nomos Rhodion Nautikos)…”** (https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Admiralty_law)

**Extracted earliest legal code/origin example:** **Rhodian law (*Nomos Rhodion Nautikos*).** (https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Admiralty_law)

### (b) One modern international maritime convention or treaty
The page names multiple modern conventions/treaties, including UNCLOS:
- Verbatim evidence (lead): **“the United Nations Convention on the Law of the Sea has been adopted by 167 countries and the European Union”** (https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Admiralty_law)

**Extracted modern convention/treaty:** **United Nations Convention on the Law of the Sea (UNCLOS).** (https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Admiralty_law)


## 3) Mathematics of paper folding (https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Mathematics_of_paper_folding)

### (a) Name of one theorem or axiom related to origami mathematics
The page lists named theorems and axioms used in origami mathematics, including Maekawa’s theorem:
- Verbatim evidence (Flat folding rules): **“Maekawa's theorem: at any vertex the number of valley and mountain folds always differ by two.”** (https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Mathematics_of_paper_folding)

**Extracted theorem/axiom:** **Maekawa’s theorem.** (https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Mathematics_of_paper_folding)

### (b) One practical engineering application of origami folding
The page describes Miura folding applied to spacecraft solar panels:
- Verbatim evidence (History): **“In 1985 Miura reported a method of packaging and deployment of large membranes in outer space, and as early as 2012 this technique had been applied to solar panels on spacecraft.”** (https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Mathematics_of_paper_folding)
- Verbatim evidence (Related problems): **“the Miura map fold is a rigid fold that has been used to deploy large solar panel arrays for space satellites.”** (https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Mathematics_of_paper_folding)

**Extracted engineering application:** **Miura-ori / Miura map fold used to deploy spacecraft/space-satellite solar panels.** (https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Mathematics_of_paper_folding)


## Synthesis essay (unifying theme; 150+ words)

A unifying theme across Cordyceps fungi, admiralty law, and the mathematics of paper folding is **the creation of reliable “control systems” for complex environments**—whether that environment is a living host, the high-uncertainty ocean, or a sheet transforming into a deployable structure.

In biology, Cordyceps exemplifies control through parasitic manipulation: the *Cordyceps* page highlights that some species **“have been making real zombies for millions of years”** in hosts such as ants, framing the fungus’s strategy as the takeover of an organism’s behaviour (https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Cordyceps). Humans, in turn, try to harness nature’s complexity for their own purposes: the same page notes cordyceps’ **“more than 1,500 years of use in Chinese medicine”** and that it **“has long been used in traditional Chinese medicine”** as a putative disease treatment (https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Cordyceps).

In the legal realm, admiralty law is a governance/control layer for maritime activity. Its roots reach back to early seafaring trade norms—**“An ancient example was the Rhodian law (Nomos Rhodion Nautikos)”**—showing that even ancient societies needed rules to stabilize ocean commerce (https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Admiralty_law). Modern shipping’s global complexity similarly demands standardization, reflected in adoption of **“the United Nations Convention on the Law of the Sea”** by **“167 countries and the European Union”** (https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Admiralty_law).

Origami mathematics offers a third angle: formal rules that control how a surface can transform. For instance, **“Maekawa’s theorem”** constrains fold parity at a vertex—**“the number of valley and mountain folds always differ by two”**—so complex crease patterns remain physically consistent (https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Mathematics_of_paper_folding). Those constraints enable engineering reliability, such as Miura-derived structures that, **“as early as 2012,”** were **“applied to solar panels on spacecraft”** (https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Mathematics_of_paper_folding). Across all three, the pattern is the same: hidden rules and constraints become tools for directing complex systems toward predictable outcomes.
final_output.action_summary: Extracted facts from three Wikipedia pages: Cordyceps—ant “zombie” behaviour (“real zombies”) and use in traditional Chinese medicine; Admiralty law—ancient Rhodian law (Nomos Rhodion Nautikos) and modern UNCLOS; Paper-folding math—Maekawa’s theorem and Miura folding used for spacecraft solar panels. Synthesized theme: control systems/rules governing complex environments.
